perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall include all information or material that has or could have commercial value or other utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this Agreement, "Confidential Information" shall include only information or material explicitly labeled as such by the Disclosing Party, regardless of its potential commercial value or utility in the business in which Disclosing Party is engaged.
perturbation.explanation: This change restricts the definition of "Confidential Information" to only explicitly labeled materials, contradicting the broader legal understanding that includes information with potential commercial value, even if unlabeled.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by requiring explicit labeling, while the UTSA protects information with commercial value regardless of labeling, as long as reasonable efforts are made to maintain its secrecy. This could lead to the unintentional disclosure of trade secrets not explicitly labeled.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines "Confidential Information" broadly, including information with commercial value. The changed text restricts this definition to only explicitly labeled information, regardless of its commercial value. This misalignment with the standard legal understanding of confidential information, where value and reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are key, constitutes a legal contradiction because the modified language deviates from legally recognized definitions regarding confidential information, making the contract potentially unenforceable in regards to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information in strictest confidence for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall hold and maintain the Confidential Information with reasonable care, but may use it for internal research purposes, even if not directly benefiting the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: This modification weakens the obligation to maintain confidentiality for the exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party, potentially allowing uses that could harm the Disclosing Party's competitive advantage.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement emphasizes the duty to maintain the confidentiality of trade secrets and prohibits using or disclosing them without authorization. This change allows use for 'internal research purposes,' potentially violating trade secret law if it leads to unauthorized disclosure or commercial exploitation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Passing off - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia Common law tort For other uses of this and related terms, see Pass (disambiguation) . The examples and perspective in this article deal primarily with the United Kingdom and do not represent a worldwide view of the subject . You may improve this article , discuss the issue on the talk page , or create a new article , as appropriate. ( February 2014 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) Passing off is a common law tort which can be used to enforce unregistered trade mark rights. The tort of passing off protects the goodwill of a trader from misrepresentation. The law of passing off prevents one trader from misrepresenting goods or services as being the goods and services of another, and also prevents a trader from holding out his or her goods or services as having some association or connection with another when this is not true. Passing off and trade mark law [ edit ] A cause of action for passing off is a form of intellectual property enforcement against the unauthorised use of the trade dress (the whole external appearance or look-and-feel of a product, including any marks or other indicia used) which is considered to be similar to that of another party's product, including any registered or unregistered trademarks. Passing off is of particular significance where an action for trade mark infringement based on a registered trade mark is unlikely to be successful (due to the differences between the registered trade mark and the unregistered mark). Passing off is a common law cause of action, whereas statutory law such as the United Kingdom Trade Marks Act 1994 provides for enforcement of registered trademarks through infringement proceedings. Passing off and the law of registered trade marks deal with overlapping factual situations, but deal with them in different ways. Passing off does not confer monopoly rights to any names, marks, get-up or other indicia . It does not recognize them as property in its own right. Instead, the law of passing off is designed to prevent misrepresentation in the course of trade to the public, for example, that there is some sort of association between the businesses of two traders. One recent example of its application by the United Kingdom Intellectual Property Office can be found in a Trade Mark Opposition Decision in 2001. [ 1 ] It was held that two brands of confectionery both named "Refreshers", one made by Swizzels Matlow and one by Trebor Bassett , which had coexisted since the 1930s, would deceive a consumer as to their source for some items but not for others. Both coexist in the marketplace. Required elements [ edit ] When coming to court, there are three elements in the tort which must be fulfilled. In Reckitt & Colman Products Ltd v Borden Inc , [ 2 ] [ 3 ] Lord Oliver reduced the five guidelines laid out by Lord Diplock in Erven Warnink v. Townend & Sons Ltd. [ 4 ] (the "Advocaat Case") to three elements: Goodwill owned by a trader Misrepresentation Damage to goodwill The plaintiff has the burden of proving goodwill in its goods or services, get-up of goods, brand, mark or the thing standing for itself. Goodwill normally develops alongside a brand name or brand association. It has been described as "the attractive force by that brings in custom" by Lord Macnaghten in the UK case IRC v Muller & Co.'s Margarine [1901] AC 217. [ 5 ] The plaintiff also has the burden of proof to show false representation (intentional or otherwise) to the public to have them believe that goods/services of the defendant are that of the Plaintiff. There must be some connection between the plaintiff's and defendant's goods, services or trade. They must show likely or actual deception or confusion by the public. It is the court's duty to decide similarity or identity of the marks, goods or services. The criteria are often: aural, visual and conceptual similarity (often applied in trademark infringement cases). For the ele
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing the Receiving Party to use Confidential Information for 'internal research purposes, even if not directly benefiting the Disclosing Party.' This weakens the original obligation to maintain confidentiality 'for the sole and exclusive benefit of the Disclosing Party.' The Law Explanation states that this change 'potentially violat[es] trade secret law if it leads to unauthorized disclosure or commercial exploitation.' Scraped Law Snippet 2, addressing passing off, while not directly on point, highlights the importance of protecting a trader's goodwill and preventing misrepresentation. Although the snippet relates to trademarks, the underlying principle of protecting a business's competitive advantage through confidential information aligns with the justification for a contradiction. The modified text creates a risk that the Receiving Party's use could harm the Disclosing Party's competitive position, undermining the original intent of strict confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information immediately if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall return to Disclosing Party any and all records, notes, and other written, printed, or tangible materials in its possession pertaining to Confidential Information within a reasonable timeframe, as determined by the Receiving Party, if Disclosing Party requests it in writing.
perturbation.explanation: This change replaces 'immediately' with 'within a reasonable timeframe, as determined by the Receiving Party,' creating ambiguity and potentially delaying the return of confidential information, which could cause harm to the disclosing party.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRCP 34 pertains to document production in litigation, it sets a standard for reasonable and timely responses. Changing 'immediately' to 'reasonable timeframe, as determined by the Receiving Party' introduces ambiguity that could be exploited to delay compliance with a court order or contractual obligation, undermining the principle of prompt response.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes (a) In General. A party may serve on any other party a request within the scope of Rule 26(b) : (1) to produce and permit the requesting party or its representative to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items in the responding party's possession, custody, or control: (A) any designated documents or electronically stored information—including writings, drawings, graphs, charts, photographs, sound recordings, images, and other data or data compilations—stored in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or (B) any designated tangible things; or (2) to permit entry onto designated land or other property possessed or controlled by the responding party, so that the requesting party may inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property or any designated object or operation on it. (b) Procedure. (1) Contents of the Request. The request: (A) must describe with reasonable particularity each item or category of items to be inspected; (B) must specify a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection and for performing the related acts; and (C) may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (2) Responses and Objections. (A) Time to Respond. The party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served or — if the request was delivered under Rule 26(d)(2) — within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. A shorter or longer time may be s
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'immediately' with 'within a reasonable timeframe, as determined by the Receiving Party.' This contradicts the legal principle of promptness and introduces ambiguity, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation also points out that this change undermines the principle of prompt response, which aligns with the general expectation of timely compliance found in legal contexts. While the scraped law snippet from FRCP 34 focuses on document production in litigation, it specifies 'a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection' which suggests that the 'reasonable' timeframe should be determined by the requesting party or established legal standards, not solely by the responding party. The changed text gives the Receiving Party the sole discretion to determine the timeframe, potentially leading to delays and non-compliance with the Disclosing Party's request and the general spirit of timely compliance.

perturbation.original_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect until the Confidential Information no longer qualifies as a trade secret or until Disclosing Party sends Receiving Party written notice releasing Receiving Party from this Agreement, whichever occurs first.
perturbation.changed_text: The nondisclosure provisions of this Agreement shall survive the termination of this Agreement and Receiving Party's duty to hold Confidential Information in confidence shall remain in effect for a period of one (1) year following the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the duration of the confidentiality obligation to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret law that protects confidential information as long as it remains a trade secret, regardless of the agreement's termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets indefinitely as long as they maintain their secrecy and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the protection to one year contradicts this principle, potentially allowing the Receiving Party to exploit trade secrets after that period, even if the information remains confidential and valuable.

perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the confidentiality obligation to one year, which contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected indefinitely as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage, as explained in the Law Explanation. The original text protects the confidential information until it no longer qualifies as a trade secret. The changed text significantly weakens this protection by imposing a one-year limit, which could allow exploitation of trade secrets even if they remain confidential and valuable after that period. While the provided snippets do not explicitly state this principle, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both highlight this conflict.

perturbation.original_text: Employee is provided notice that an individual shall not be held criminally or civilly liable under any federal or state trade secret law for the disclosure of a trade secret that is made (i) in confidence to a federal, state, or local government official, either directly or indirectly, or to an attorney; and (ii) solely for the purpose of reporting or investigating a suspected violation of law; or is made in a complaint or other document filed in a lawsuit or other proceeding, if such filing is made under seal.
perturbation.changed_text: Employee is provided notice that an individual shall not be held criminally or civilly liable under any federal or state trade secret law for the disclosure of a trade secret that is made (i) in confidence to a federal, state, or local government official, either directly or indirectly; and (ii) solely for the purpose of reporting or investigating a suspected violation of law, provided that such disclosure does not result in financial gain for the individual.
perturbation.explanation: Adding the condition that the disclosure must not result in financial gain alters the whistleblower protections. While the original clause provides immunity for reporting violations, this change introduces a subjective element that could deter legitimate whistleblowing activities due to fear of being accused of seeking financial gain.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA provides broad immunity for disclosing trade secrets to government officials or in court filings for reporting violations of law. The added condition regarding 'financial gain' contradicts the intent of the DTSA, which is to encourage reporting without fear of reprisal, regardless of potential incidental benefits. This modification could have a chilling effect on whistleblowing.

Exceptions to prohibitions (a) In General .-This chapter does not prohibit or create a private right of action for- (1) any otherwise lawful activity conducted by a governmental entity of the United States, a State, or a political subdivision of a State; or (2) the disclosure of a trade secret in accordance with subsection (b). (b) Immunity From Liability for Confidential Disclosure of a Trade Secret to the Gov
perturbation.justification: The modified text adds a condition ('provided that such disclosure does not result in financial gain for the individual') that is not present in the original clause regarding whistleblower protections under trade secret law. The original clause provides immunity for reporting violations, while the change introduces a subjective element that could deter legitimate whistleblowing activities. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 18 USC 1833, discusses exceptions to prohibitions related to trade secrets, and while it doesn't explicitly forbid adding conditions like 'no financial gain,' the added condition undermines the intent of encouraging reporting without fear of reprisal. The Law Explanation suggests that this condition contradicts the intent of the DTSA.
